displayTitle: When Did It Form? The Three Discontinuity Families

beats.narration: Every part carries a history, and a discontinuity can enter at any chapter. When a flaw formed predicts where it hides.

payload.title: WHEN DID IT FORM?
payload.sub: A discontinuity's origin predicts where it sits and what it looks like.

beats.narration: We sort them into three families: inherent, born in the melt; processing, created during manufacturing; and service-induced, earned in the field.
Follow the timeline: the melt leaves porosity, inclusions, shrinkage. Forging and welding add laps, bursts, weld cracks. Service grows fatigue cracks and corrosion.

payload.heading: The material life cycle
payload.note: ORIGIN PREDICTS WHERE IT SITS AND HOW IT LOOKS

beats.narration: One trap: rolling doesn't erase ingot flaws. Porosity and inclusions flatten into laminations in plate, seams along bar, and stringers. Still inherent.
Make it a habit: before you look at the part, ask what has happened to it. Read the part's history before you read the part.

payload.sub: That's the difference between looking and inspecting.
